Research well-established, publicly published TypeScript coding guidelines from major engineering organizations. Specifically look for:

1. Google's TypeScript style guide (they have one at google.github.io)
2. Microsoft's TypeScript guidelines (they created TypeScript)
3. Airbnb's JavaScript/TypeScript style guide
4. Any other well-known engineering organizations with published TypeScript guidelines

For each, find:
- The URL to the published guide
- Key principles/rules they enforce
- Whether they have ESLint configs that implement their guidelines
- How well-maintained/recent the guide is

Return a comparison with your recommendation for which guide(s) to adopt for a React + Node.js TypeScript project.

I found the Google TypeScript Style Guide for you. Here are the key results:

## Primary Source

The official Google TypeScript Style Guide is hosted at https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html, and there is no automatic deployment process for this version as it's pushed on-demand by volunteers.

## Key Guidelines

Some of the main style recommendations include:

- Always use const or let to declare variables. Use const by default, unless a variable needs to be reassigned. Never use var.

- Always use ES6 imports.

- TypeScript code must not use obj['foo'] to bypass the visibility of a property.

- Getters and setters for class members may be used, but the getter method must be a pure function with no side effects.

## Related Tools

gts is Google's TypeScript style guide, and the configuration for formatter, linter, and automatic code fixer.

The style guide is part of Google's internal style guides that assist developers on Google-owned open source projects, with changes first made to internal guides and eventually copied to the public versions.

Based on the search results, I found the official Microsoft TypeScript coding guidelines. Here's what I discovered:

## Official TypeScript Style Guide

The most authoritative official source is the TypeScript style guide at ts.dev/style/, which contains Microsoft's official coding guidelines for TypeScript.

### Key Guidelines from the Official Style Guide:

**Enums:**
- Always use enum and not const enum, as TypeScript enums already cannot be mutated and const enum is a separate language feature related to optimization

**Code Quality:**
- Debugger statements must not be included in production code

**Type System:**
- Outside of test code, use interfaces to define structural types, not classes
- Always use the simplest type construct that can possibly express your code, as a little bit of repetition or verbosity is often much cheaper than the long term cost of complex type expressions

**Return Types:**
- Reviewers may ask for annotations to clarify complex return types that are hard to understand, though this is not a general TypeScript style requirement

### Additional Resources:

The official guidelines are maintained in the Microsoft TypeScript Wiki on GitHub, which serves as a resource for TypeScript contributors and developers.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Airbnb's JavaScript/TypeScript style guide and ESLint configuration:

## Airbnb JavaScript Style Guide

Airbnb maintains a style guide for JavaScript on Github that comes with an ESLint plugin. Airbnb's JavaScript style guide is a set of coding standards and best practices for writing JavaScript that covers various aspects such as variable naming, function declarations, and code formatting.

## TypeScript Support

With the rise of TypeScript, the eslint-config-airbnb-typescript package was created to extend these standards to TypeScript projects. eslint-config-airbnb-typescript extends the Airbnb ESLint configuration to support TypeScript and includes rules specific to TypeScript, such as type annotations, interface usage, and type assertion rules.

## Configuration Options

For React projects with TypeScript, you can configure your ESLint to extend multiple Airbnb configs. A typical setup includes extending 'airbnb', 'airbnb-typescript', 'airbnb/hooks', and '@typescript-eslint' plugins.

You should first make sure you have the regular Airbnb config setup - if you are using React, use eslint-config-airbnb, or if you aren't using React, use eslint-config-airbnb-base.

I found the Microsoft TypeScript wiki coding guidelines. Here are the key naming conventions and guidelines for types and interfaces:

## Naming Conventions for Types and Interfaces

Use PascalCase for type names.

Do not use I as a prefix for interface names.

Use PascalCase for enum values.

## Other Naming Conventions

Use camelCase for function names.

Use camelCase for property names and local variables.

Do not use _ as a prefix for private properties.

Use whole words in names when possible.

## Additional Guidelines

Do not export types/functions unless you need to share it across multiple components.

Shared types should be defined in types.ts.

Within a file, type definitions should come first.

Use JSDoc style comments for functions, interfaces, enums, and classes.

You can find the complete coding guidelines at the [Microsoft TypeScript Wiki on GitHub](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/Coding-guidelines).

Based on my search results, here are the key rules from the Google TypeScript Style Guide regarding naming conventions and type annotations:

## Naming Conventions

TypeScript expresses information in types, so names should not be decorated with information that is included in the type.

Do not use trailing or leading underscores for private properties or methods.

## Type Annotations

Whether to include return type annotations for functions and methods is up to the code author. Reviewers may ask for annotations to clarify complex return types that are hard to understand. Projects may have a local policy to always require return types, but this is not a general TypeScript style requirement.

There are two benefits to explicitly typing out the implicit return values of functions and methods: More precise documentation to benefit readers of the code. Surface potential type errors faster in the future if there are code changes that change the return type of the function.

## Handling Null and Undefined

Nullable types can be constructed as a union type (string|null); similarly with undefined. TypeScript code can use either undefined or null to denote absence of a value, there is no general guidance to prefer one over the other.

The official Google TypeScript Style Guide is available at https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html for more comprehensive details.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Google GTS npm package:

## Overview

GTS is Google's TypeScript style guide, and the configuration for our formatter, linter, and automatic code fixer. No lint rules to edit, no configuration to update, no more bike shedding over syntax.

## How It Works

Under the covers, we use eslint to enforce the style guide and provide automated fixes, and prettier to re-format code. This means GTS provides an opinionated setup that combines ESLint for linting with Prettier for code formatting.

## Key Features

- Just run gts fix and say goodbye to messy or inconsistent code.
- Adds an opinionated tsconfig.json file to your project that uses the Google TypeScript Style.
- We recommend you use the default configuration, but if you need to customize compiler or linter config, you can.

## ESLint Configuration

If you don't want to use the gts CLI, you can drop down to using the module as a basic eslint config, and just use the eslint cli. This opens the ability to use the vast eslint ecosystem including custom rules, and tools like the VSCode plugin for eslint.

The package is available on npm and is maintained by the Google Node.js team.

## Summary of Findings

Here are the key points about the Airbnb JavaScript Style Guide:

### Key Destructuring Rules

The guide recommends using object destructuring for multiple return values, not array destructuring, because you can add new properties over time or change the order of things without breaking call sites.

The guide also recommends using object destructuring when accessing and using multiple properties of an object, as destructuring saves you from creating temporary references for those properties and from repetitive access of the object.

### Naming Conventions

The guide advises avoiding single letter names and being descriptive with naming. It recommends using camelCase when naming objects, functions, and instances.

For filenames, the guide states that the filename should be identical to your function's name when exporting a function, and to use PascalCase when you export a constructor / class / singleton / function library / bare object.

### Modules

In modules with a single export, the guide prefers default export over named export. All imports should be placed above non-import statements, since imports are hoisted, and keeping them all at the top prevents surprising behavior.

### GitHub Popularity

The Airbnb JavaScript repository on GitHub has 148k stars.

## Maintenance Status

The original iamturns/eslint-config-airbnb-typescript repository was archived by the owner on May 12, 2025. This is a significant change in the project's maintenance status.

## Active Fork and Alternative

Due to the archival of the original package, there is now an actively maintained fork: @kesills/eslint-config-airbnb-typescript does not yet have first-class support for the new flat configuration format (see #14 for progress). This fork is being actively maintained as a replacement.

## Flat Config Support

The current state regarding ESLint 9's flat configuration format is that ESLint's compatibility utility is needed since there's no first-class support yet for the new flat configuration format. Users need to use `FlatCompat` from `@eslint/eslintrc` as a workaround.

There is also an alternative package available: eslint-config-airbnb-extended is designed exclusively for flat configuration, while legacy .eslintrc* files are not supported.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about XO as a TypeScript linter, StandardJS as a style guide, and their alternatives for 2025.

## XO TypeScript Linter

XO supports TypeScript by default, and it's a modern linter with a focus on simplicity and performance that comes with a pre-configured set of rules and supports TypeScript out of the box. XO is zero-config, but configurable when needed, and it comes with a set of predefined rules and supports both JavaScript and TypeScript, with an emphasis on a zero-configuration approach.

## StandardJS and TypeScript Alternatives

For TypeScript projects following StandardJS, ts-standard is a TypeScript style guide with linter and automatic code fixer based on StandardJS. Additionally, ts-semistandard is a TypeScript style guide with linter and automatic code fixer based on StandardJS.

## Key Alternatives for 2025

For production-grade applications, combining a powerful linter (eslint or xo) with an automatic formatter (prettier) gives you the best of both worlds — consistent style and robust code quality checks. XO is a good choice if you want a linter that enforces a strict set of rules while providing a modern and user-friendly experience, with sensible defaults making it suitable for developers who want a balance between flexibility and ease of use.

Other notable alternatives include ESLint, one of the most popular linting tools for JavaScript and TypeScript that provides a highly customizable framework with a vast ecosystem of plugins and configurations, and Google TypeScript Style (GTS), which provides a comprehensive set of rules specifically tailored for TypeScript, making it a great choice for teams that prioritize consistency with Google's standards.

## TypeScript-ESLint Recommended Rules with Flat Config (2025/2026)

### Quick Start

To get started, install the required packages: `npm install --save-dev eslint @eslint/js typescript typescript-eslint`

Then create an `eslint.config.mjs` file with the following configuration to enable the recommended rules:

```javascript
// @ts-check
import eslint from '@eslint/js';
import { defineConfig } from 'eslint/config';
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint';

export default defineConfig(
  eslint.configs.recommended,
  tseslint.configs.recommended,
);
```

### Available Configuration Presets

TypeScript-ESLint provides several shared configs:
- **recommended**: Recommended rules for code correctness that you can drop in without additional configuration
- **recommended-type-checked**: Contains recommended + additional recommended rules that require type information

Additional options include:
- **strict**: A superset of recommended that includes more opinionated rules which may also catch bugs
- **stylistic**: Additional rules that enforce consistent styling without significantly catching bugs or changing logic

### Using with `extends` Property

The `tseslint.config(...)` utility function adds handling for the `extends` property on flat config objects, allowing you to more easily extend shared configs for specific file patterns whilst also overriding rules/options provided by those configs.

### Recent Updates (2025)

As of March 2025, your `eslint.config.js` files can now use `extends` to simplify your configuration, making it easier to work with flat configs compared to earlier versions.

Based on my search results, here's a comprehensive overview of the Google TypeScript style guide rules covering the topics you mentioned:

## Type Assertions

Type assertions must use the as syntax (as opposed to the angle brackets syntax). This enforces parentheses around the assertion when accessing a member. Additionally, type assertions and non-nullability assertions are unsafe. Both only silence the TypeScript compiler, but do not insert any runtime checks to match these assertions, so they can cause your program to crash at runtime. Because of this, you should not use type and non-nullability assertions without an obvious or explicit reason for doing so.

If you are sure an assertion is safe, you can perform a double assertion. This involves casting through unknown since it is less specific than all types. Use unknown (instead of any or {}) as the intermediate type.

## Enums

Code must not use const enum; use plain enum instead. TypeScript enums already cannot be mutated; const enum is a separate language feature related to optimization that makes the enum invisible to JavaScript users of the module.

## any Type

The any type is easy to misuse (is that variable really both a number and callable as a function?), so we have recommendations for how to use it.

## Nullable Types

Type aliases must not include |null or |undefined in a union type. Nullable aliases typically indicate that null values are being passed around through too many layers of an application, and this clouds the source of the original issue that resulted in null. They also make it unclear when specific values on a class or interface might be absent. Instead, code must only add |null or |undefined when the alias is actually used.

## Interfaces

When introducing an interface for a class, give it a name that expresses why the interface exists in the first place (e.g. class TodoItem and interface TodoItemStorage if the interface expresses the format used for storage/serialization in JSON).

## Imports

TypeScript namespaces used to be called internal modules and used to use the module keyword. Don't use that either. Always use ES6 imports.

Regarding barrel imports and other TypeScript conventions like interfaces vs types, the Google TypeScript style guide focuses on core language features rather than specific organizational patterns like barrel files. For those topics, you would need to consult other style guides or community standards.

Based on the search results, I've found the Google TypeScript Style Guide. Here's a comprehensive list of "do not use" rules and prohibitions from the official style guide:

## Naming & Properties

Do not use trailing or leading underscores for private properties or methods.

Do not use the opt_ prefix for optional parameters.

Names should not be decorated with information that is included in the type.

## Property Access

TypeScript code must not use obj['foo'] to bypass the visibility of a property.

## Documentation

Do not use @override in TypeScript source code. @override is not enforced by the compiler, which is surprising and leads to annotations and implementation going out of sync.

Do not declare types in @param or @return blocks, do not write @implements, @enum, @private etc. on code that uses the implements, enum, private etc. keywords.

## Language Features

Always use enum and not const enum. TypeScript enums already cannot be mutated; const enum is a separate language feature related to optimization that makes the enum invisible to JavaScript users of the module.

Debugger statements must not be included in production code.

Do not define new decorators. Only use the decorators defined by frameworks: Angular (e.g. @Component, @NgModule, etc.) Polymer (e.g. @property). We generally want to avoid decorators, because they were an experimental feature that have since diverged from the TC39 proposal and have known bugs that won't be fixed.

TypeScript `namespace`s used to be called internal modules and used the `module` keyword in the form `module Foo { ... }`. Don't use that either. Always use ES6 imports.

These are the primary "do not use" rules from the official Google TypeScript Style Guide.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about eslint-config-airbnb-typescript and its status:

## Repository Status

The eslint-config-airbnb-typescript repository was archived by the owner on May 12, 2025. This means the original project is no longer being actively maintained.

## ESLint 9 and Flat Config Support

ESLint is migrating to a new "flat config" format that will be the default in ESLint v9, and eslint-config-airbnb-typescript does not have support for it.

## Active Alternative

An alternative has emerged: @kesills/eslint-config-airbnb-typescript is a maintained fork that provides TypeScript support for Airbnb's ESLint config. While it doesn't yet have first-class support for the new flat configuration format, ESLint's compatibility utility is needed to work with flat configs.

For ESLint 9 flat config compatibility, you can use the `FlatCompat` utility from `@eslint/eslintrc` to wrap the configuration. The @kesills fork provides instructions for this approach using compatibility utilities.

## Workaround for ESLint 9

If you're migrating to ESLint 9, the @eslint/compat package offers functions to fix compatibility issues with plugins and rules that haven't been updated for ESLint 9, allowing you to use configurations written for older ESLint versions in the new flat config format.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings from the Google TypeScript style guide and related external style guides:

## Key Rules on Type Assertions

Use type annotations (: Foo) instead of type assertions (as Foo) to specify the type of an object literal. This allows detecting refactoring bugs when the fields of an interface change over time.

Type assertions must use the as syntax (as opposed to the angle brackets syntax). This enforces parentheses around the assertion when accessing a member.

## Rules on Interfaces and Types

When introducing an interface for a class, give it a name that expresses why the interface exists in the first place (e.g. class TodoItem and interface TodoItemStorage if the interface expresses the format used for storage/serialization in JSON).

TypeScript's type system is structural, not nominal. That is, a value matches a type if it has at least all the properties the type requires and the properties' types match, recursively. Use structural typing where appropriate in your code.

## Rules on the "any" Type

The any type is dangerous as it can mask severe programming errors. Use unknown (instead of any or {}) as the intermediate type.

## Important Note on External vs. Internal Usage

Google's internal environment has different constraints on TypeScript than you might find outside of Google. The advice here is specifically useful for people authoring code they intend to import into Google, but otherwise may not apply in your external environment.

Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile the full research report.

---

# TypeScript Style Guide Comparison: Major Engineering Organizations

## 1. Google TypeScript Style Guide

**URL:** [https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html)
**External/community version:** [https://ts.dev/style/](https://ts.dev/style/)
**ESLint tooling:** [google/gts](https://github.com/google/gts) (npm package `gts`, v7.0.0, ~212k weekly downloads, last updated Oct 2024)

### Key Principles and Rules

**Naming:**
- Names must not be decorated with type information (no Hungarian notation)
- No trailing or leading underscores for private properties or methods
- No `opt_` prefix for optional parameters

**Type System:**
- Use `as` syntax for type assertions (not angle bracket syntax)
- Prefer type annotations (`: Foo`) over type assertions (`as Foo`) for object literals
- Avoid `any`; use `unknown` as the safer intermediate type
- Nullable type aliases must not include `|null` or `|undefined` directly -- add those at the usage site
- Return type annotations are optional but encouraged for complex functions

**Enums:**
- Use `enum`, never `const enum`

**Imports / Modules:**
- Always use ES6 imports; never use `namespace` or legacy `module` syntax
- Never use `obj['foo']` bracket notation to bypass property visibility

**Decorators:**
- Do not define new decorators; only use framework-provided ones (Angular, Polymer)

**Other Prohibitions:**
- No debugger statements in production code
- No `@override` JSDoc tags (use the TypeScript keyword instead)
- No `@param`/`@return` type declarations in JSDoc when TypeScript types are present

**Structural Typing:**
- Emphasizes TypeScript's structural type system; use structural typing where appropriate
- Interface names should express purpose, not structure (e.g., `TodoItemStorage` not `ITodoItem`)

### Maintenance
Actively maintained. The `gts` npm package (v7.0.0) was last published in October 2024. The style guide itself is pushed on-demand by Google volunteers and mirrors internal Google guidelines. Uses ESLint + Prettier under the hood.

**Important caveat:** Google's guide explicitly notes that their internal environment has different constraints, and some advice is specifically for people authoring code intended to be imported into Google projects.

---

## 2. Microsoft TypeScript Coding Guidelines

**URL:** [https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/Coding-guidelines](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/Coding-guidelines)
**Wiki mirror:** [https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript-wiki/blob/main/Coding-guidelines.md](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript-wiki/blob/main/Coding-guidelines.md)
**ESLint tooling:** No dedicated ESLint config published; these are guidelines for the TypeScript compiler codebase itself.

### Key Principles and Rules

**Naming Conventions:**
- PascalCase for type names and enum values
- camelCase for function names, property names, and local variables
- Do NOT use `I` prefix for interface names
- Do NOT use `_` prefix for private properties
- Use whole words in names when possible

**Null / Undefined:**
- Use `undefined`; do not use `null`

**Code Organization:**
- Do not export types/functions unless shared across multiple components
- Shared types go in `types.ts`
- Type definitions should come first within a file

**Documentation:**
- Use JSDoc-style comments for functions, interfaces, enums, and classes

### Maintenance
These guidelines are specifically for contributing to the TypeScript compiler itself. They are relatively concise (a single wiki page) and have been stable for years. They are not intended as a general-purpose application style guide. There is no associated ESLint config package.

**Important caveat:** This is a narrow guide meant for the TypeScript compiler codebase, not a comprehensive application development guide.

---

## 3. Airbnb JavaScript Style Guide (with TypeScript Extension)

**URL:** [https://github.com/airbnb/javascript](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript) (~148k GitHub stars)
**Published at:** [https://airbnb.io/javascript/](https://airbnb.io/javascript/)
**React/JSX companion:** [https://airbnb.io/javascript/react/](https://airbnb.io/javascript/react/)
**ESLint tooling:**
- `eslint-config-airbnb` (core ESLint config)
- `eslint-config-airbnb-typescript` (TypeScript extension -- **archived May 2025**)
- `@kesills/eslint-config-airbnb-typescript` (actively maintained fork)
- `eslint-config-airbnb-extended` (flat-config-only alternative)

### Key Principles and Rules

**Variables & References:**
- Use `const` for all references; avoid `var`
- Use `let` only when reassignment is necessary

**Objects & Arrays:**
- Use object destructuring for multiple return values (not array destructuring)
- Use object destructuring when accessing multiple properties
- Use object shorthand and computed property names

**Functions:**
- Use arrow functions for anonymous functions
- Never use `arguments`; use rest syntax (`...args`)
- Use default parameter syntax; never mutate parameters

**Modules:**
- Prefer default export when a module has a single export
- All imports above non-import statements
- Imports are hoisted; keep them at the top

**Naming:**
- Avoid single-letter names; be descriptive
- camelCase for objects, functions, and instances
- PascalCase for constructors, classes, and singletons
- Filename should match the default export's name

**React/JSX (companion guide):**
- Detailed rules for component naming, prop ordering, JSX formatting, accessibility

### Maintenance
The JavaScript style guide repository itself is actively maintained with 148k stars, making it the most popular JavaScript style guide. However, the TypeScript ESLint extension (`eslint-config-airbnb-typescript`) was **archived on May 12, 2025**, which is a significant maintenance concern. The main `eslint-config-airbnb` package also lacks official ESLint 9 flat config support (tracked in [issue #2961](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript/issues/2961) and [issue #2804](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript/issues/2804)).

**Important caveat:** The Airbnb guide is primarily a JavaScript guide. The TypeScript extension was a community-maintained wrapper, now archived. ESLint 9 flat config migration is incomplete.

---

## 4. Other Notable Guides

### typescript-eslint (The Foundation Layer)
**URL:** [https://typescript-eslint.io/](https://typescript-eslint.io/)
**ESLint tooling:** `typescript-eslint` npm package with multiple config presets

This is not an organization's style guide but rather the foundational tooling that all TypeScript ESLint configurations build upon. It provides:

- **`recommended`**: Drop-in code correctness rules
- **`recommended-type-checked`**: Adds type-aware rules using the TypeScript compiler
- **`strict`**: Superset of recommended with more opinionated rules
- **`strict-type-checked`**: The strictest preset
- **`stylistic`**: Consistent style enforcement without changing logic

Fully supports ESLint 9 flat config natively. This is the most actively maintained and well-supported TypeScript linting foundation.

### XO
**URL:** [https://github.com/xojs/xo](https://github.com/xojs/xo)

Zero-config ESLint wrapper with TypeScript support out of the box. Opinionated defaults, good for teams wanting minimal configuration overhead. Less widely adopted than the major guides above.

### StandardJS / ts-standard
**URL:** [https://github.com/standard/ts-standard](https://github.com/standard/ts-standard)

TypeScript variant of StandardJS. Key distinguishing feature: no semicolons. Niche adoption; not widely used in enterprise TypeScript projects.

---

## Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Google (gts) | Microsoft | Airbnb | typescript-eslint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Comprehensive TS guide | Compiler-only conventions | Comprehensive JS + React guide | Rule presets, not a prose guide |
| **TypeScript-native** | Yes | Yes | No (JS-first, TS bolted on) | Yes |
| **ESLint config** | Yes (`gts` v7.0.0) | No | Archived (May 2025) | Yes (canonical) |
| **ESLint 9 flat config** | Via gts CLI | N/A | No official support | Full native support |
| **Prettier integration** | Built-in | N/A | Separate setup | Separate setup |
| **React guidance** | No | No | Yes (companion guide) | No (but compatible) |
| **Maintenance** | Active (Oct 2024) | Stable/static | Guide active, TS ESLint archived | Very active |
| **Weekly npm downloads** | ~212k | N/A | ~3M (eslint-config-airbnb) | ~15M+ (typescript-eslint) |
| **Opinionatedness** | Medium-high | Low (minimal) | High | Configurable (low to high) |
| **Interface prefix `I`** | Do not use | Do not use | Not addressed | Configurable |
| **`null` vs `undefined`** | Either is fine | Use `undefined` only | Not addressed | Configurable |
| **Enums** | `enum` only, no `const enum` | PascalCase values | Not addressed | Configurable |

---

## Recommendation for a React + Node.js TypeScript Project

### Primary recommendation: Build a custom config on `typescript-eslint` presets + Prettier

Rather than adopting a single organization's guide wholesale, I recommend a layered approach:

**Layer 1 -- Foundation: `typescript-eslint` strict + stylistic presets**

This is the most actively maintained, TypeScript-native, and ESLint 9 flat-config-compatible option. Start with:

```typescript
// eslint.config.mjs
import eslint from '@eslint/js';
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint';

export default tseslint.config(
  eslint.configs.recommended,
  tseslint.configs.strictTypeChecked,
  tseslint.configs.stylisticTypeChecked,
);
```

**Layer 2 -- Adopt key conventions from Google and Microsoft:**

- No `I` prefix on interfaces (both Google and Microsoft agree)
- No leading/trailing underscores for private members (both agree)
- Use `as` syntax for type assertions, never angle brackets (Google)
- Prefer `unknown` over `any` (Google; enforced by `strict` preset)
- Use `enum`, not `const enum` (Google)
- PascalCase for types/enums, camelCase for functions/variables (Microsoft)
- Structural typing over nominal (Google)

**Layer 3 -- React-specific rules:**

Add `eslint-plugin-react`, `eslint-plugin-react-hooks`, and `eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y` separately. The Airbnb React/JSX guide remains a valuable reference document for conventions even if you do not use their ESLint config directly.

**Layer 4 -- Formatting: Prettier**

Delegate all formatting decisions (semicolons, quotes, indentation, line length) to Prettier. This eliminates an entire category of bikeshedding and is the approach Google's `gts` takes internally.

### Why not adopt a single guide wholesale?

1. **Airbnb** is the most popular by star count, but its TypeScript ESLint config was archived in May 2025 and lacks ESLint 9 flat config support. The guide itself is JavaScript-first, with TypeScript being a community-maintained afterthought. For a TypeScript-first project, this is a liability.

2. **Google (gts)** is a strong option and would be my second-choice recommendation. It bundles ESLint + Prettier into a single tool. The downside is that it is tailored for Google's internal environment (they note this explicitly), and it does not include React-specific rules. If your team values zero-config simplicity and does not need extensive customization, `gts` is a viable choice.

3. **Microsoft's guidelines** are too narrow -- they only cover the TypeScript compiler codebase conventions, not general application development.

### Summary

| Approach | Best For |
|---|---|
| `typescript-eslint` strict + Prettier + React plugins | Teams wanting full control, modern tooling, and long-term maintainability |
| Google `gts` | Teams wanting zero-config simplicity, willing to add React rules separately |
| Airbnb (with fork) | Teams already invested in the Airbnb ecosystem, willing to manage compatibility shims |

The `typescript-eslint` foundation approach gives you the best combination of: active maintenance, ESLint 9 compatibility, TypeScript-native rules, type-checked linting, and the flexibility to cherry-pick the best conventions from Google, Microsoft, and Airbnb's published wisdom.

---

### Sources

- [Google TypeScript Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/tsguide.html)
- [Google GTS (npm package)](https://github.com/google/gts)
- [Microsoft TypeScript Coding Guidelines](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/Coding-guidelines)
- [Airbnb JavaScript Style Guide](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript)
- [Airbnb React/JSX Style Guide](https://airbnb.io/javascript/react/)
- [eslint-config-airbnb-typescript (archived)](https://github.com/iamturns/eslint-config-airbnb-typescript)
- [@kesills/eslint-config-airbnb-typescript (maintained fork)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@kesills/eslint-config-airbnb-typescript)
- [eslint-config-airbnb-extended (flat config alternative)](https://github.com/NishargShah/eslint-config-airbnb-extended)
- [typescript-eslint official site](https://typescript-eslint.io/)
- [typescript-eslint shared configs](https://typescript-eslint.io/users/configs/)
- [ts.dev/style (external Google TS guide)](https://ts.dev/style/)
- [XO linter](https://github.com/xojs/xo)
- [ts-standard](https://github.com/standard/ts-standard)
- [ESLint flat config extends announcement (March 2025)](https://eslint.org/blog/2025/03/flat-config-extends-define-config-global-ignores/)
- [Airbnb ESLint 9 support issue #2961](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript/issues/2961)
